architecture_summary: This paper is a comprehensive survey of Transformer techniques for multimodal learning. It reviews Vanilla Transformer, Vision Transformer, and multimodal Transformers from a geometrically topological perspective, and organizes multimodal Transformer methods by application scenarios and by technical challenges.
novel_contributions: the first comprehensive review of the state of Transformer based multimodal machine learning
a geometrically topological perspective on Transformers in a multimodal context
a two-tier structured taxonomy based on the application and challenge dimensions
a systematic review of Vanilla Transformer, Vision Transformer, and multimodal Transformers
a review of multimodal Transformer applications via multimodal pretraining and specific multimodal tasks
a summary of common challenges and designs shared by multimodal Transformer models and applications

key_claims.claim: This survey presents a comprehensive review of Transformer techniques oriented at multimodal data, covering background, model taxonomy, applications, shared challenges, and open problems.
The paper claims to be the first comprehensive review of the state of Transformer based multimodal machine learning.
Transformers can work in a modality-agnostic way and self-attention can be understood as graph style modelling in a multimodal context.
The survey contributes a two-tier taxonomy based on application and challenge dimensions for Transformer based MML.

Transformer-based multimodal pretraining models pretrained on large-scale multimodal corpora clearly outperform other competitors in a wide range of multimodal down-stream tasks and can achieve zero-shot generalization ability.
The paper argues that multimodal Transformers face bottlenecks in generative tasks, fine-grained tasks, efficiency, robustness, universality, and interpretability.

The survey highlights that cross-modal interactions in multimodal Transformers are implemented through self-attention and its variants, including early summation, early concatenation, hierarchical attention, and cross-attention.
